chief_complaint: Progressive joint pain, chronic fatigue, and butterfly-shaped facial rash worsening over 3 months
symptoms: Bilateral polyarthralgia affecting MCPs, PIPs, wrists, and knees
Malar (butterfly) rash across nose and cheeks
Photosensitivity with rash exacerbation on sun exposure
Recurrent oral ulcers (painless, on hard palate)
New onset peripheral edema (bilateral lower extremities)
Decreased urine output noted over past 2 weeks

diagnosis_codes.description: Glomerular disease in systemic lupus erythematosus
Systemic lupus erythematosus, organ or system involvement unspecified
Nephrotic syndrome with unspecified morphologic changes

requested_procedure.name: Renal Ultrasound (Complete, with Doppler)
requested_procedure.reason: Evaluate renal parenchyma and vascularity in setting of suspected lupus nephritis progression with rising creatinine and proteinuria

prior_treatments: Prednisone taper (60mg → 10mg over 8 weeks) — completed 1 month ago
Naproxen 500mg BID PRN — for joint pain
clinical_notes: 42-year-old female with established SLE (diagnosed 8 months ago, meeting 6/11 ACR classification criteria) presenting with signs of disease flare and new renal involvement. ANA persistently positive at high titer with positive anti-dsDNA confirming active disease. Complement levels are consumed (low C3 and C4) indicating active immune complex-mediated disease. New finding of significant proteinuria (UPC 1.8) with rising creatinine suggests lupus nephritis — Class III or IV cannot be excluded without further workup. Renal ultrasound is the essential first step before potential renal biopsy to evaluate kidney size, echogenicity, and vascular flow. This is time-sensitive given concern for rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis. Patient has been compliant with hydroxychloroquine but disease has progressed despite therapy, supporting need for urgent evaluation and likely escalation to mycophenolate or cyclophosphamide.
